title: Revolution & Reflection: Campus Experiences in Art, History, and Ideas
description: Look for your guide, President George Washington, as you drop in to visit these three revolutionary destinations:The Lilly Library presents The Declaration of Independence: The Motives, The Moment (https://libraries.indiana.edu/lilly-library/declaration-independence).The Sidney and Lois Eskenazi Museum of Art showcases George Washington's portrait by Gilbert Stuart.  IU Auditorium is the caretaker of Thomas Hart Benton's iconic murals (https://www.iuauditorium.com/about-us/benton-murals-organ-and-dailey-art-collection), titled The Social History of Indiana.Spend an afternoon with us. Or a whole day. This self-guided offering is designed to allow you to create your own educational experience. Visit all locations in one trip or come back to campus multiple times. Each Fine Arts Plaza destination has different operating hours; please consult them carefully when planning your trip. All locations are open Tuesday–Friday between 10 a.m. and 5 p.m.  Check our online event calendar at go.iu.edu/america-250 (https://go.iu.edu/america-250) for special events and additional campus celebrations of America's 250th.  To schedule a tour for groups of students in grades 2-12, see Field Trip Tours: Revolution & Reflection (https://artmuseum.indiana.edu/visit/museum-tours/tour-types.html).

title: The Declaration of Independence: The Motives, the Moment
description: The Declaration of Independence: The Motives, The Momentexamines a crucial time in American history through the collections of the Lilly Library. The first printing of the Declaration of Independence, one of only twenty-six copies known today, is the centerpiece of the exhibition. Printed immediately for distribution, it is the source of all revolutionary era reprintings. The famous hand-written version was an official formality created later, signed in August, and stored away.Books, pamphlets, newspapers, prints, and letters allow viewers to explore key questions about this moment in time. How did a people proud to be in the British empire come to believe independence was possible or even desirable? What ideas and historical events did they draw on to justify resistance to Great Britain? What kinds of communication networks would be crucial for circulating the Declaration and countless other revolutionary documents throughout the colonies?
source: IU Arts & Humanities Institute, IU Bloomington Libraries

title: The Whole World in a Book: Celebrating Madeline Kripke's Dictionary Collection
description: When Kripke died in 2020, she left behind a remarkable collection of over 20,000 dictionaries, as well as a rich manuscript archive about dictionaries, her life, and her collecting. In 2021, the Lilly Library acquired the collection, keeping it intact and available for all to study. The spring 2026 exhibition The Whole World in a Book: Celebrating Madeline Kripke's Dictionary Collection shares some of the collection publicly for the first time, curated by IU Provost Professor of English Michael Adams (https://english.indiana.edu/about/faculty/adams-michael.html) and Lilly Library Executive Associate Director Erika Dowell.  Madeline Kripke believed that dictionaries are an especially useful lens for observing culture. She focused on slang and other marginalized language, and she charted the roles of dictionaries in everyday life. She collected, not merely to possess, but to share what she saw with scholars and the public, and The Whole World in a Book realizes her ambition to do so.  This exhibition is on display until July 24, 2026. For the Lilly Library's daily hours, please visit the website (https://libraries.indiana.edu/lilly-library).

title: Moon Over Buffalo - Auditions
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
Audition Notice:
Moon Over Buffalo
by Ken Ludwig
The Brown County Playhouse and Director Steve Booker will hold auditions at the Brown County Playhouse on March 16 and 17 from 6:30pm – 8:30pm. You can arrive anytime up to 8:00. Actors will read from audition sides, which will be provided when you arrive.
Important Dates: Production dates are May 29, 30, 31, and June 5, 6. Rehearsals will typically be Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday evenings, beginning March 30, in Nashville.
Show Overview: It is the summer of 1953, and George and Charlotte Hay, former Broadway stars, have taken their run-down touring company to Buffalo, New York. In Buffalo they have the intention of running “Cyrano de Bergerac” and “Private Lives” in repertory, all the while grumbling about missed Hollywood opportunities. But on-stage harmony is compromised when George performs an off-stage infidelity, impregnating the company’s ingenue. When Charlotte learns of this, she prepares to run away with the family lawyer, sending lightweight George on a grief-stricken drinking binge. Meanwhile, it  turns out that Frank Capra is headed to town on a talent scouting mission looking to hire the couple for his swashbuckling Scarlet Pimpernel epic. As a result, the Hay family — including scornful, deaf mother-in-law Ethel, determinedly practical daughter Rosalind, and dashing actor Paul, Rosalind’s ex-boyfriend — must work overtime to get sloppy drunk George into his Cyrano hat and nose…. or is it his Elyot Chase smoking jacket? With the entrance of Rosalind’s fiancé, anxious TV weatherman Howard — one man with two mistaken identities — and Richard Maynard, the wealthy lawyer hoping to lure Charlotte away to his mansion — the confusion only intensifies.
Character Overview: The ages shown below are the script suggestions. However, the director encourages anyone interested to audition for the show, even if your age doesn’t match up with certain roles.

George Hay, a traveling actor, age 50s
Charlotte Hay, George’s wife and actress in his company, age 50s
Ethel, Charlotte’s nearly deaf mother, Age 70s
Rosalind, The Hays’ daughter, who left the stage to lead a “normal” life, Age 20s – 30s
Howard, A TV weatherman and Rosalind’s fiancé, Age 20s – 30s
Eileen, An actress in the company who was George’s ‘one-night stand”, Age 20s
Paul, Stage manager for the company and Rosalind’s ex-fiancé, Age 20s – 30s
Richard, A love-sick lawyer who is courting Charlotte, Age 50s

description: Looking for a welcoming space to crochet, knit, embroider, quilt, and explore other fiber arts? title: OWLS -16.5 Miles Bolin and Rockport
location: Sherwood Oaks Christian Church, 2700 E Rogers Rd, Bloomington, IN 47401, USA
description:
16.5 Miles Owls Bolin and Rockport

Non-members should sign up for a free trial membership, good for up to one month, and which includes electronically signing a liability waiver, prior to participation.

The “Older, Wiser, and a Little Slower” group emphasizes moderate rides and a route planned in advance that is posted on the ride calendar. This is a no drop ride for a range of intermediate riders—it is not a beginner’s ride. Average pace is 12-15 mph which includes hills. Regroup points are discussed in advance. Early season rides are shorter, and rides get longer as the season progresses. Peak season rides average 25–35 miles, depending on available daylight. Rides depart promptly at 5:45. Front and rear lights are encouraged.

Questions? Contact Gail Morell.

Sign Up Link: https://pickleballtournaments.com/tournaments/columbus-spring-open-2026-presented-by-selkirk?fbclid=IwY2xjawPuzr1leHRuA2FlbQIxMQBzcnRjBmFwcF9pZBAyMjIwMzkxNzg4MjAwODkyAAEe4vKzXen1klvUUM5x5g0PbigFAjEHPjo-yE2QsXECkXIRknqB6bLEm1hr418_aem_yTCPUmxtD7lOjPpeGHQ3bw

This has quickly become one of southern Indiana’s favorite spring tournaments, and for good reason:
• Indoor play - no weather worries
•  8 dedicated courts
•  Easy access - less than ½ mile off I-65
•  Hotels + food right across the street

• Men’s, Women’s & Mixed Doubles

title: Mindful Makers
description:
Hosted by: Health Promotion

Join us for Mindful Makers, a weekly event built around being creative, meeting new people and feeling more confident in yourself and your wellness. Each week has a theme - come for one or come for all! We're here to have fun and make cool things. January 21: Semester Vision Boards Picture your new semester! Create a vision board that will motivate you to achieve this semester. Learn some tips on how to set great goals to help you achieve that vision.   January 28: Meditation and Aromatherapy Ever been curious about aromatherapy and how it works? Here's your opportunity to learn more! While you're here, we'll also practice deep breathing techniques, progressive relaxation and guided visualization, and other simple techniques that don't require any special skills or equipment   February 4: Crochet the Stress Away Whether you're a crochet expert or have never picked up a hook in your life, we have a place for you! Join us, learn basic crochet stitch

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: Senior Movie Series: Poms
location: Brown County Playhouse, 70 S. Van Buren St, Nashville, IN, 47448, United States
description:
Meredith Funeral Home, in partnership with the historic Brown County Playhouse, is excited to
offer a Free Monthly Movie Series for those aged 60 and over. Enjoy a classic film with friends, free
popcorn, and a soda—all in the comfort of the historic Brown County Playhouse!

When:
MAR 19, 2026 – Poms
APR 16, 2026 – Book Club
May 21, 2026 – Senior Moment
Time: 2:00 p.m. Doors open at 1:00 p.m.
Where: Historic Brown County Playhouse, 70 S Van Buren St. in Nashville, IN
No ticket is necessary, and admission is free for those 60 and over. For those under 60, admission is just
$7.

This initiative is part of the Meredith family’s ongoing commitment to giving back to the community. Bill
and Jamie Meredith, along with their dedicated staff, are excited to create a monthly opportunity for
seniors to connect with others and enjoy a time of fellowship.
Celebrating 50 years of exceptional customer service, Meredith Funeral Home remains locally
owned and operated. Throughout these years, they have earned the trust of the community by
providing compassionate, dignified services at an affordable price.
We invite you to join us for an afternoon of movies, community, and memories. We look forward to
seeing you at the Brown County Playhouse Senior Movie Series!

—————————————-

POMS is an uplifting comedy about a woman (played by Keaton) who moves into a retirement community and starts a cheerleading squad with her fellow residents, including Pam Grier and Jacki Weaver.

title: What do people ask when they first see a Canang Sari and feel confused?
description: When people first see a canang sari, they often feel a mixture of curiosity and confusion. Many wonder, “What is this small, intricate offering made of flowers and leaves?” or “Why is it placed in a specific corner or on the ground?” Others might ask, “Does it have a spiritual purpose, and who is it meant for?” The colours, scents, and careful arrangement can feel mysterious, leaving observers questioning their meaning and significance. Some even think it’s purely decorative, unaware of the deep intention behind each element. Similarly, just as the canang sari is a daily practice to invite gratitude and harmony, Joy Potential teaches that joy is cultivated intentionally and with awareness. People often ask, “How can I feel joy consistently in my life?” or “Is joy something I am born with, or can I learn it?” Just as each canang sari is created with care to honour the divine and the present moment, Joy Potential shows that small, consistent practices can awaken our inner light. Observers might also ask, “Do I need to understand the rituals to experience the benefit?” In the same way, Joy Potential invites individuals to explore joy without needing to grasp all the science or methods at once. Both the canang sari and Joy Potential emphasise presence, intention, and transformation. People often feel an initial hesitation, unsure if they belong in this space of mindfulness and heart-centred practice. Joy Potential encourages stepping in, exploring, and allowing the process to work, just as one would approach a canang sari with curiosity and respect. Each small action, whether placing a flower in a basket or practising a joyful habit, contributes to a larger sense of peace and fulfilment. Finally, just like the mystery behind the canang sari draws people to learn more, Joy Potential inspires individuals to reconnect with their authentic joy, understand its practices, and integrate them into daily life.

title: Weekly Lilly Library puzzle tour
location: Lilly Library - Room Number: Slocum Room
description: Your expert guide, our Curator of Puzzles Andrew Rhoda (https://libraries.indiana.edu/andrew-rhoda), will introduce you to the collection, mechanical puzzles and the collection's donor, Jerry Slocum. You will learn about the various categories of mechanical puzzles and their history. You will also see highlights from the collection on exhibition in the Slocum Room. In addition, there will be a group of puzzles from the collection selected by the Curator for visitors to try as part of the tour.  Non-flash photography is permitted, seating is available in the Slocum Room throughout the tour, and the building is wheelchair accessible. The tour generally lasts a little less than one hour and is open to all. No reservation or RSVP is required for this free tour - just drop in!

title: Berlin Soirée – A Showcase of Emerging Voices from Indiana University's Jacobs School of Music
description: Please join us for a classical soirée featuring performances by emerging talents from the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music.      Enjoy an intimate evening that will showcase the exceptional vocal abilities of IU's rising stars. With a diverse program of timeless works, each performance reflects the dedication, artistry, and passion of these gifted young singers. This recital offers a wonderful opportunity to hear the voices of tomorrow as they take the stage today.      This is a free event. Attendance to this event is capped and decided on a first-come, first-served basis. Please register below.           Meet the Performers  Haocheng Du, Tenor          Tenor Haocheng Du is pursuing a Doctor of Music in Voice Performance degree at the Jacobs School of Music, studying under the guidance of Heidi Grant Murphy. Originally from Wuhan, China, he completed his undergraduate and graduate studies at the Wuhan Conservatory of Music. He studied under Professor Leiming He during his gradute studies, his undergradute mentors were Professor Yongxi Chen and Lecture Jinping Li. Du has earned several accolades, including being a finalist in the 2023 Hubei Regional Final of the 14th China Golden Bell Award for Music and the 10th Hubei Golden Bell Music Award. He also won first place in the Bel Canto Group of the Wuhan Conservatory of Music Cup Competition. More recently, he was a semifinalist in the 2024 China Little Golden Bell First National Vocal Arts Exhibition. At IU, Du performed La Bohème (Rodolfo) in 2025.        Theo Harrah, Bass          A bass from Louisville, Kentucky, Theo Harrah is a second-year master's student studying under the tutelage of Jane Dutton. His previous credits at Jacobs include the Second Armed Man in The Magic Flute, Dick Deadeye in H.M.S. Pinafore, Siroco in L'Étoile, José Tripaldi in Ainadamar, Frère Laurent in Roméo et Juliette, Judge Turpin in Sweeney Todd, Zuniga in Carmen, and Sheldon Anapol in the world premiere of The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay in collaboration with the Metropolitan Opera. Additionally, he performed in the choruses of Falstaff, Highway 1, USA, Candide, The Merry Widow, and The Marriage of Figaro. He has also been featured as a soloist in Mozart's Requiem, Schütz's Musikalische Exequien, and the Howells Requiem. Outside of Jacobs, Harrah has been seen as Colline in La Bohème and Simone in Gianni Schicchi at the Canto Program, Dean of the Faculties in Massenet's Cendrillon at Cedar Rapids Opera, and on concert stages worldwide performing everything from Schubert to Verdi. In 2024, he was a finalist in the Meistersinger competition in Graz, Austria. In 2025, Harrah was in the Santa Fe Opera apprentice program for singers, where he sang in the choruses of La Bohème and Rigoletto and sang the role of Count Ceprano. This summer, he looks forward to participating in the Merola Opera program in San Francisco, where he will be singing Lakai in Strauss's Ariadne auf Naxos as well as covering Truffaldino in the same production.        Esther Jo, Mezzo-Soprano          Mezzo-soprano Esther Jo, from Seoul, South Korea, is pursuing an Artist Diploma with a Performance Fellowship and full scholarship under the tutelage of Jane Dutton at the Jacobs School of Music. She also recently completed her Performer Diploma, which includes a Performer's Certificate, with the fellowship and scholarship. With Indiana University Opera Theater, she has portrayed the title role of Carmen, Madame de la Haltière in Cendrillon, La Zia Principessa in Suor Angelica, and Olga in Eugene Onegin. She has also appeared in opera workshops, performing Reine Gertrude in Hamlet, Giovanna Seymour in Anna Bolena, and Carmen in Carmen, and will perform Charlotte in Werther. Jo was named a finalist in the prestigious Georgina Joshi Foundation Graduate Fellowship Competition. She was also selected to participate in a masterclass with Stuart Skelton. She will further expand her artistic career in Europe as a Faculty-Nominated and Competition Winner participant in the Berlin Performance Program. This summer, she will join Opera Maine as a Studio Artist, covering Gertrude in Roméo et Juliette, and performing Khanh in Bound. In Korea, Jo studied under Locky Chung and Su-gil Park, performing Maddalena in Rigoletto (a coproduction of Hanyang University and the Seongdong Cultural Foundation), Dorabella in Così fan tutte, and Dritte Dame in Die Zauberflöte, among other roles. As an alto soloist, she has appeared in Charpentier's Te Deum, Vivaldi's Gloria in D Major, Britten's A Ceremony of Carols, and Schütz's Die sieben Worte Jesu Christi am Kreuz. She further refined her artistry through masterclasses with Mutsumi Moteki and as a voice fellow at Yewool Music Stage. Jo graduated summa cum laude with a Bachelor of Music from Hanyang University, receiving multiple merit-based full scholarships, an exclusive teaching certification, and ranking among the top three students in the voice department. She continued her studies in the Master of Music program with full scholarship and research assistantship.        Brittany Rose Weinstock, Soprano          Originally from Los Angeles, soprano Brittany Rose Weinstock is a Performer's Diploma student at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music studying under Carol Vaness, where she also earned her Master's degree. She earned her B.A. from Pepperdine University, studying under Louise Lofquist. This summer, Ms. Weinstock will be a Resident Artist with Teatro Nuovo, covering Zaida in Il Turco in Italia and study-covering Donna Anna in Don Giovanni. Indiana University performances include the title role in Massenet's Cendrillon, Micaëla in Carmen, and Helen in The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay, a co-production with the Metropolitan Opera. Previous engagements include Music on Site (Contessa, Le nozze di Figaro), Aspen Music Festival (Musetta cover, La Bohème), Central City Opera (Anna Maurrant cover, Street Scene), and the Sherrill Milnes Savannah VOICE Festival. Other credits include H.M.S. Pinafore (Josephine), Into the Woods (Jack's Mother), and Le nozze di Figaro (Contessa) with Pepperdine University. Brittany Rose has received recognition in competitions such as the Opera Birmingham Competition (Finalist), Camille Coloratura Awards (Semi-Finalist), NSAL Indiana Vocal Competition (3rd Place), Carolyn Bailey Argento and Loren L. Zachary Competitions (Finalist), and Laffont Competition (Encouragement Award). Ms. Weinstock is also an avid scholar and a 2025-26 recipient of the Georgina Joshi Travel Grant, which allowed her to travel to Central Europe to research Jewish Music of the Holocaust.        Samuel Wetzel, Baritone          Samuel Wetzel is pursuing a Performer Diploma in Solo Performance, studying with Jane Dutton. Hailing from Erie, Pennsylvania, he has most recently sung Count Almaviva in Jacobs' production of Le Nozze di Figaro, Melisso in Alcina and the baritone solos in Vaughn Williams' Dona Nobis Pacem with the University Chorale. He is a regular soloist with the Bloomington Bach Cantata Project, and performs with local early music ensembles, most recently performing BWV 106 with the Second Presbyterian Church of Louisville featuring Guy Fishman. He earned a master's degree from the University of Colorado at Boulder (CU), where he sang a number of roles, including Pa in Mizzy Mazoli's Proving Up, Ford in Verdi's Falstaff, Pandolfe in Massenet's Cendrillon, and Marcello in Puccini's La Bohème. He also participated in the CU New Opera Works Festival, where he worked with Gene Scheer (2024) and Tom Cipullo (2023) to workshop their new operas. He participated in the Opera Steamboat program, singing Brundibar in Brundibar. He recently won the Colorado Bach Ensemble's Annual Competition for Young Artists and performs regularly with them. He earned a bachelor's degree in music at the Baldwin Wallace Conservatory of Music, where he sang roles in their operas and participated in the annual Bach Festival.        Allan Armstrong, Faculty PianistFaculty Pianist - Allan Armstrong          Pianist and vocal coach Allan Armstrong is associate professor of music in voice at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, where he specializes in art song literature and opera coaching. He is also the official accompanist of the Metropolitan Opera National Council Auditions for both the Colorado/Wyoming District and the Rocky Mountain Region. From 2017 to 2020, he was a postdoctoral scholar and visiting assistant professor in chamber and collaborative music at the Jacobs School. He was previously a member of the applied piano faculty at the University of Texas Rio Grande Valley, where he codirected the nationally award-winning Bravo Opera Company. Armstrong has been a principal production pianist and coach at Eugene Opera, Opera Colorado, St. Petersburg Opera, Opera on the Avalon, Sugar Creek Opera, Tel Aviv Summer Opera Program, and Opera Tampa. He has taught on the faculty of the Sherrill Milnes Savannah Voice Festival and the International Vocal Arts Institute, in Blacksburg, Virginia. In 2005, he coached and recorded the newly revised version of Béla Bartók's Bluebeard's Castle under the direction of the composer's son, Peter Bartók.​ In 2010, Armstrong was a featured solo pianist in a recital of the complete solo piano works of Pulitzer Prize-winning composer David Del Tredici at New York University Steinhardt. Armstrong earned a Doctor of Musical Arts in Collaborative Piano degree from the University of Colorado Boulder. He also earned a Master of Music degree in Chamber Music and a Bachelor of Arts degree from the University of South Florida, where he studied with Svetozar Ivanov and Robert Helps. Armstrong holds professional memberships in the College Music Society and the National Association of Teachers of Singing (NATS). In 2019, he was chosen to participate in the acclaimed NATS Intern Program at the New England Conservatory.

title: Wilderness First Aid Courses
description:
Hosted by: Bradford Woods

Our Wilderness First Aid (WFA) courses will give you the skills needed to respond to medical emergencies when help is far away. This is a 2-day/16-hour course that features extensive hands-on learning opportunities. Students will walk away with a 2-year WFA certification from Stonehearth Open Learning Opportunities (SOLO), which is one of the oldest wilderness medicine organizations in the world. Students may also add on CPR modules and/or re-certify for their SOLO Wilderness First Responder (WFR) certification. Courses start at $275. Cost includes course materials and lunch on Saturday and Sunday. Overnight lodging from Friday–Sunday may be available for participants on a first-come, first-serve basis. title: Programme 4: Make the Scene
description: $15.00 - Tickets for the 7 PM screening of PROGRAMME 4: MAKE THE SCENE 2026 • US • dir. Rachel Lichtman Saturday, March 21st at Blockhouse Bar (205 S College) $15 dollars | 21+ Pre-show grooviness: 7pm Screening: 8pm From fondue parties to cocktail lounges to art galleries to cinemas, Programme 4: Make the Scene is an analog, local broadcast television happening designed to be collectively experienced in person, an immersive, wall-to-wall carpeted presentation that transports you to an aspirational world of tomorrow, today. With creator Rachel Lichtman, special guest stars, Rhodes pianos, complimentary matchbooks and more. Escape to a groovy, aspirational world of Programme 4 with Special Guest Stars: Mike Adams as your Master of Ceremonies, Jared Cheek playing soft sounds & Mystic Moods, and Stephen Deusner in-conversation with Programme 4 creator Rachel Lichtman . The happening vibes start "After 6" with pre-show grooviness before the feature presentation at 8pm. Make the Scene will include a feature-length video presentation of Programme 4 , plus creator Rachel Lichtman, special guest stars, talk show-style Q&A’s, Rhodes pianos, and more. Larger productions will evoke broadcast network upfronts of the 60’s and 70’s, a surreal sales presentation featuring a cavalcade of stars and the satirical UHF television and AM radio of Golden Sands. Rachel Lichtman is a visual artist, comedy writer, and conjurer of groovy worlds. Her work has featured a cavalcade of notable actors, comics and musicians, and she has produced, directed and written documentaries, live stage shows, full-length music shows, art installations, title sequences and over four billion music videos. She is currently producing more deep-shag Programme 4 magic and figuring out how to install those lucite stairs that light up when you walk on them. More information about Programme 4: https://www.programme4.tv/

title: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead by Tom Stoppard

In Tom Stoppard's best-known work, this Shakespearean Laurel and Hardy finally get a chance to take the lead role, but do so in a world where echoes of Waiting for Godot resound, where reality and illusion intermix, and where fate leads our two heroes to a tragic but inevitable end.

title: Understanding Autism
location: First Christian Church Bloomington 205 E Kirkwood
description: A great deal of misinformation has been spread – particularly by our current President and Secretary of Health and Human Services – about the reasons for the rapid rise in diagnosed cases of autism spectrum disorder over the past half-century. Professors Emeritus Tim Londergan and Steve Vigdor will review the expansion of diagnostic criteria for the neurodevelopmental condition(s) known as autism, and how these diagnostic changes have contributed to the rise. They will also show that the totality of epidemiological studies establish no link between autism and vaccines and at most a weak association (not necessarily causal) with prenatal Tylenol usage. Extensive twin studies have established a dominant role for genetics and a minor role for environmental conditions in contributing to autism. Ongoing brain imaging studies of people on the autism spectrum are revealing atypical connectivity issues that are associated with some autistic behaviors.

title: Spring 2026 Colloquium Series with Paulo Ramos
description: The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies is proud to present the continuation of its Colloquium Series for Spring 2026.  We welcome Paulo Ramos, former postdoc at the Center for Research on Race & Ethnicity in Society, current visiting scholar of Ostrom Workshop.  Paulo's research project Bodies of Water ("Corpos d'água") explores environmental racism and Black women's relationships to water. Part of his work offers artistic performance that delves into conceptual realms through poetry and corporeal expression, and he has published a volume of poetry also entitled Bodies of Water. Paulo's performance invites a sensory experience while reflecting on environmental racism and transcending racial and class boundaries. Focusing on the connection between water and Black women, Paulo's work highlights the draining of dreams and lives from Black bodies by the state, a situation that dates back to colonial Brazil. The situation Paulo examines constitutes an ongoing cycle of violence deeply rooted in the legacy of slavery and entrenched within institutional frameworks.  Abstract: PhD dissertation Bodies of Water: etnographY of Black Poetics of Feeling-Doing (2025) is a multidisciplinary work that combines art, literature, and anthropology of the senses and images transformed into emotions. Corpos d'água is about Paulo's mother, grandmother, and aunt and the relationship these Black women had with the waters of the Bahia River, an aquatic current between the river, the mangrove, and the sea which becomes the Atlantic Ocean. The work-research analyzes these geographies as spaces of escape, freedom, and energy connected to Black women mothers. Paul's art is a meditation on water and Black women in literature and art as a diasporic force that, even though traversed by the rupture and violence of colonialism, does not erase ancestry or Black life.  The CLACS Spring 2026 Colloquium meets Mondays from February through April, with faculty and guest speakers engaging Latin America and the Caribbean through research on history, culture, language, politics, and the arts. Check the CLACS Calendar (https://events.iu.edu/clacs/) for details.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU La Casa Latino Cultural Center

title: Guest Lecture – JB Dyas, "Living the Dream: How to Get a Job in Jazz Education"
description: Students, Sign up here >JB Dyas has been a leading force in jazz education for more than three decades. As Vice President for Education and Curriculum Development at the Herbie Hancock Institute of Jazz at UCLA, he oversees major outreach initiatives includingJazz in America. An Indiana Universityalumnuswith a Ph.D. in Music Education, Dyas studied with and was closely mentored by legendary jazz educator David Baker. He previously served as Executive Director of the Brubeck Institute and Director of Jazz Studies at Miami-Dade College and New World School of the Arts. Dyas has presented workshops and performances worldwide and is aDownBeatJazz Education Achievement Award recipient.

title: Breaking Tradition: America's 250th Birthday!
location: LOWELL E. BAIER HALL/IU MAURER SCHOOL OF LAW - Room Number: 125
description: Professor Randy Kozel of the University of Notre Dame Law School, joined by Professor William Popkin, will explore how we, as law students, lawyers, and judges, interpret our Constitution in light of its history and tradition. What relationship does the history and traditions approach to constitutional interpretation have to other interpretive methods? How does this system of interpretation influence the shaping of American law today? Join us to celebrate America's 250th birthday!  BuffaLouie's will be provided for all attendees

Participants will answer a series of trivia questions. For every incorrect answer, they must eat a hot wing with an increasing heat level. In the hidden high-security government laboratory where she works, lonely Elisa (Sally Hawkins) is trapped in a life of isolation. Elisa’s life is changed forever when she and co-worker Zelda (Octavia Spencer) discover a secret classified experiment. Michael Shannon rounds out a stellar cast also including Richard Jenkins, Michael Stuhlbarg, and Hoosier-born Doug Jones. title: It's On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop
description:
Hosted by: Office for Sexual Violence Prevention and Victim Advocacy

The It’s On Us: Bystander Intervention Workshop is a 90-minute, audience-driven bystander intervention program that teaches students the skills needed to help in situations involving alcohol and drug use that could lead to a sexual assault. Students practice helping in real-life scenarios and discuss how alcohol and drug use influences a person’s ability to give and get consent to engage in sexual activity with another person.    It's On Us is  required  for all first-year and transfer undergraduate students.   You must attend one session to fulfill the requirement.    Instructions:    Those who arrive more than 5 minutes after the start time will NOT be allowed into the session and will need to attend a different session   If this is your assigned workshop but you cannot attend  it in its entirety , please RSVP

title: Philharmonic Orchestra – Jeffery Meyer, conductor; Jenny Bahk, cello soloist
description: Enjoy this performance from almost anywhere in the world viaLIVE@jacobs!Repertoire  Dvořák: Cello Concerto in B Minor, Op. 104 (1895)Rachmaninoff: Symphonic Dances, Op. 45 (1940)About the Conductor  Distinguished for insightful performances, visionary programming, and wide-ranging collaborations, conductor Jeffery Meyer has captivated audiences around the globe. He is professor of orchestral conducting at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music as well as artistic partner with the Northwest Sinfonietta, one of the northwest United States' most vibrantly creative ensembles. Artistic director of the St. Petersburg Chamber Philharmonic for two decades, his work with the orchestra was noted for its breadth and innovation. His programming has earned three ASCAP Awards for Adventurous Programming and two Vytautas Marijošius Memorial Awards. A champion of contemporary music, Meyer imagines the orchestra experience as a forum for story, emotion, and civic dialogue. His projects have paired symphonic works with multimedia visionaries and diverse cultural collaborators. His recordings span repertoire from baroque to the present; his most recent, Illuminations: Music of Narong Prangcharoen, was released by Albany Records in 2023. Recent highlights include a multimedia Rite of Spring and Petrushka with the Thailand Philharmonic; Carlos Simon's Graffiti with renowned graffiti artists; a theatrical symphonic concert developed in collaboration with Daniel Bernard Roumain and Marc Bamuthi Joseph; Laura Kaminsky's Piano Concerto with Ursula Oppens (Cedille Records); a semistaged production of Nkeiru Okoye's Invitation to a Die-In; and multiple collaborations with the American Composers Orchestra's EarShot program. Recent and upcoming engagements include the City Chamber Orchestra of Hong Kong, Los Angeles Ballet, Xalapa Symphony, Orquesta Juvenil Universitaria Eduardo Mata, American Composers Orchestra, Texas Festival Orchestra at Round Top, and returns to the Grossman Ensemble, Sichuan Symphony, and Thailand Philharmonic. Passionate about working with young musicians and music education, Meyer is an active adjudicator, guest clinician, and master class teacher. Prior to his appointment at the Jacobs School, he led acclaimed orchestral programs at Arizona State University and Ithaca College. He has given master classes throughout the United States, Canada, Europe, and Asia. Meyer holds degrees in piano and composition and earned his Doctor of Musical Arts in Piano Performance with Gilbert Kalish at Stony Brook University. Meeting URL: https://liveatjacobs.music.indiana.edu/

title: Eiteljorg Artist In Residence Visiting Lecture: JJ Otero
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART
description: 1–2 p.m. Artist talk, Learn more about JJ's artistic journey, creative philosophy, and evolving practice, followed by an open Q+A session, Martin Commons, second floor2–4 p.m. Enjoy hands-on artist programming and a rare look at selected objects from the museum's collections.    JJ Otero (Navajo/Hopi) brings a unique perspective to contemporary Native art. After early success as an award-winning musician and later as a painter and cradleboard maker, he shifted his artistic focus to jewelry and metalsmithing. Since 2018, his work has been featured at prominent Native art markets, including the Heard Museum Art Market, SEEDS Art Market, and the Santa Fe Indian Market.  Guided by the belief that"This moment is perfect and there's nothing lacking,"JJ's approach to texture, stamping, and form demonstrates both technical rigor and an evolving artistic voice.All of these activities are free and open to the public. This program is co-organized by the IU First Nations Educational and Cultural Center and the Eskenazi Museum of Art, with support from the Eiteljorg Museum of American Indians and Western Art.

title: Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Yaron Weissberg
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Tocqueville
description: Bio  Yaron Weissberg is a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Research Group for International Politics and Conflict Research. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Political Economy from the University of Lucerne and a Master's degree in Comparative and International Studies from ETH Zürich and the University of Zürich. He completed his Ph.D. at ETH Zrich's International Conflict Research Group, focusing on the intersection of natural resources and conflict. His research examines how the transition to cleaner forms of energy can reduce risks associated with resource-based conflicts, while also exploring new risks that might emerge.Title  Digging Out of Trouble? The Development Potential of Green Minerals in Conflict-Affected RegionsAbstract  The global transition to renewable energy requires substantial increases in green technology mineral (GTM) extraction, including lithium, cobalt, and graphite. While mineral wealth may fuel conflict, resources can also provide employment during violence and revenues for post-conflict reconstruction. This paper examines whether GTMs exhibit such stabilizing characteristics in conflict-affected regions. We argue that GTMs differ fundamentally from "traditional" conflict resources. Unlike oil and diamonds, GTMs lack concentrated value to generate large revenues for armed actors during conflict. Furthermore, their bulky nature requires substantial infrastructure investments, creating local economic spillovers that benefit the local economy and population more broadly. However, GTM extraction remains highly vulnerable to large-scale conflict due to infrastructure dependence and insufficient revenues for firms to justify continued operations under extreme insecurity. Using PRIO-GRID data and UCDP conflict measures for the post-Cold War period, we analyze spatial variation in mining activity and nighttime light emissions across conflict intensities. We find that GTM sites demonstrate economic resilience during localized violence but experience severe disruption during civil wars, with substantial variation by extraction mode. These findings clarify conditions under which resource extraction economically stabilizes rather than destabilizes conflict-affected regions, contributing to our understanding of the green energy transition's security implications.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems. Meeting URL: https://iu.zoom.us/j/87017579442

title: Ostrom Workshop | Research Series: Jorge Canabal
location: The Ostrom Workshop, 513 N PARK AVE - Room Number: Toqueville
description: Bio  Jorge Acevedo Canabal, MD, is a visiting scholar at the Ostrom Workshop and co-Principal Investigator of the "Team Building for the Cyber-Epidemiology Center" project, which receives funding from an IU 2030 Emerging Frontiers Grant. His past relevant work involves clinical medicine and research focused on novel pathogens and vulnerable populations, disaster response, healthcare crises response, and cybersecurity in healthcare.Title  Counting the Invisible: Expansion and Application of Technological Hazards' Classification to Document Human Harm in Healthcare, Public Health, and BeyondAbstract  Despite growing reliance on digital technologies and connected devices to improve public safety, public health, and healthcare delivery, there exists an identified but not comprehensively described gap in the classification and documentation of failure modes that can result in human harm. This gap is notable precisely because external cause documentation frameworks in vital statistics and clinical settings are not new: existing tools successfully capture harm attributable to natural disasters such as hurricanes, floods, and earthquakes. It is in the specific domain of technological hazards involving digital systems that the most significant classificatory gaps remain. These failure modes can be broadly described as accidental, adversarial, or as the result of cascading effects of failures in the infrastructure upon which the system of interest relies.  During this talk, the presenter will discuss (1) how adoption and adaptation of existing frameworks and tools from several fields of study, including epidemiology, health informatics, and patient safety science, can support the development of cross-disciplinary functional definitions; (2) how adaptation of Ostrom's IAD framework can be useful for systematically identifying, at different levels of observation, relevant feedback loops that can inform targeted responses; and (3) challenges that must be addressed for the development and implementation of applied research that improves current understanding of technological failures' relationship to human harm, and that can inform further development of technologies and interventions that mitigate risk.About the Ostrom Workshop Research Series  Learn about innovative research by Graduate Students and Visiting Scholars as they advance the mission of the Ostrom Workshop, building upon the theme of governance to understand and address major societal problems. Meeting URL: https://iu.zoom.us/j/81861799060

title: 21JPSI/IKS **SPECIAL EVENT** "China's Weaponization of Trade: Korea, Japan, and the Need for Collective Resilience" (Victor Cha (Georgetown/CSIS))
location: Global & International Studies Building - Room Number: GA 0001 (Shreve Auditorium)
description: Public event featuring a public talk and discussion/Q&A with Dr. Victor Cha (https://www.csis.org/people/victor-cha) (Georgetown/CSIS; former NSC Director for Asia) based on his new book: China's Weaponization of Trade: Resistance through Collective Resilience (https://cup.columbia.edu/book/chinas-weaponization-of-trade/9780231564205/) (Columbia University Press, 2026).Co-hosted/co-organized by 21JPSI and IKS. Co-sponsored by the Department of Political Science.About the book:  In recent years, China and the United States have each turned economic interdependence into an instrument of coercion, using their dominant positions in international trade to push states and firms to comply with their political goals. What is distinctive about this form of economic pressure, and how can other countries fight back?  This groundbreaking book explores the weaponization of economic interdependence and its implications for the international order through a wealth of new and original data on China's economic statecraft. Victor D. Cha, Ellen Kim, and Andy Lim examine how and in what ways the United States and China have deployed economic coercion, focusing on China's extensive use of this tactic over the past three decades. They analyze a vast data set that includes more than 600 cases of China's economic bullying of states, companies, and individuals in North America, Asia, and Europe. Cha, Kim, and Lim propose a multilateral strategy of "collective resilience" to counter intimidation, showing how targeted states can band together, leverage trading relationships, and threaten retaliation. Synthesizing new insights from unique trade data with international security expertise, this timely book sheds new light on how China exercises economic power—and it provides a playbook to deter bullies and rebalance the global order.About the speaker:Dr. Victor Cha is president of the Geopolitics and Foreign Policy Department and Korea chair at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS). He is also a distinguished university professor and professor of government at Georgetown University. From 2021 to 2025, he was appointed by the Biden administration to serve on the Defense Policy Board in an advisory role to the secretary of defense. From 2004 to 2007, he served on the National Security Council (NSC), where he was responsible for Japan, Korea, Australia, New Zealand, and Pacific Island nations. Dr. Cha was the U.S. deputy head of delegation at the Six Party Talks and received two outstanding service commendations during his tenure at the NSC. He is the author of nine books, including the award-winning Alignment Despite Antagonism: The United States–Korea–Japan Security Triangle (Stanford University Press, 1999), which won the 2000 Ohira Book Prize, and The Impossible State: North Korea, Past and Future (Ecco, 2012), selected by Foreign Affairs as a "Best Book on the Asia-Pacific for 2012." His other books are Nuclear North Korea: A Debate on Engagement Strategies (Columbia University Press, 2003); Beyond the Final Score: The Politics of Sport in Asia (Columbia University Press, 2009); Powerplay: Origins of the American Alliance System in Asia (Princeton University Press, 2018); Korea: A New History of South and North (Yale University Press, 2023); The Black Box: Demystifying the Study of Korean Unification and North Korea (Columbia University Press, 2024); North Korea's Sea-Based WMD Capability (Bloomsbury, 2025); and China's Weaponization of Trade: Resistance through Collective Resilience (Columbia University Press, 2026). Dr. Cha is a two-time Fulbright scholar and a former Olin fellow at Harvard University, as well as a former Hoover, Stanford Center for International Security and Cooperation, and Koret fellow at Stanford University. He currently serves on the editorial boards of ten academic journals and is coeditor of the Contemporary Asia book series at Columbia University Press. He serves on the boards of the National Endowment for Democracy and the Korea Society and is a senior fellow at the George W. Bush Institute. Dr. Cha received his PhD, MIA, and BA from Columbia University and a BA with honors from Oxford University.Co-sponsored by 21JPSI, the Institute for Korean Studies, and the East Asia and the World speaker series. Unless otherwise noted, all 21JPSI events are 100% in-person.
source: IU Asian Culture Center, IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Kelley School of Business

title: Panhellenic Study Tables 03/25
description:
Hosted by: Panhellenic Association

Panhellenic is excited to host their first-ever study tables! These will be bi-weekly sessions from 6-8 p.m. on Wednesdays hosted for women to collaborate with others in similar fields or majors.    WHAT:  Each specific session will be dedicated to a specific class or exam that is occurring later in the week or the following week. For example, if there is an accounting exam the following week, we will host a study table specifically for that accounting exam!   HOW: Each chapter's academic affairs/learning contact in their chapter will post what exam/class the study session is for, and any Panhellenic woman is welcome to come. Please keep an eye out for their messages for room updates each week!   WHEN: Every other Wednesday starting 03/25, from 6-8 p.m. The location for each table will be sent out via your Academic Affairs liaison in each chapter!   We look forward to seeing you there :)

description:
Date: March 25
Time: 6 to 7 p.m.
Location: IMU Maple Room

Help Start the Lotus Student Club at IU
Are you passionate about music, culture, community, and creativity?
The Lotus Education & Arts Foundation is looking for Indiana University students who want to help launch the official Lotus Student Club at IU.
This student-led group will help connect campus life with the global arts community through opportunities like:

supporting the Lotus World Music & Arts Festival
working with artists from around the world
helping produce cultural events and workshops
gaining hands-on experience in arts, media, and event production
building a welcoming community around music, culture, and creative exchange

Whether you love music, arts, production, storytelling, or just meeting interesting people from around the world — there’s a place for you here.
This is a chance to shape the club from the ground up and create something meaningful on campus.
Interested in helping start it?
Reach out or join our interest meeting: Let’s build something beautiful together.

title: Open Mic Night at The Comedy Attic
location: The Comedy Attic,123 S Walnut St, Bloomington, IN 47408, USA
description:
Every Wednesday at 8PM is Comedy Attic Open Mic Night. Bloomington has one of the best local comedy scenes in the country, and it all starts with our open mic night!

Each comedian is asked to do no more than five minutes of completely original material. While we do not care about foul language in your set, we do not allow racist, homophobic, transphobic or rape/female bashing jokes. If you have to ask if it's racist, homophobic, transphobic or a rape joke, it probably is.

Comedians that wish to participate are asked to e-mail comedyattic@gmail.com mailto:comedyattic@gmail.com . Newcomers and any participants who are not paid professionals will be required to bring audience members to see them. This is non-negotiable. So if you plan on signing up, be sure to invite as many friends as you can. Good luck!

title: The Philadelphia Orchestra
description: The world-renowned Philadelphia Orchestra strives to share the transformative power of music with the widest possible audience and to create joy, connection, and excitement through music in the Philadelphia region, across the country, and around the world. For their Bloomington performance, they'll be led by principal guest conductor Marin Alsop, a pioneering force in classical music and the first woman to serve as the head of major orchestras in the United States, South America, Austria, and Great Britain.     Alsop will lead a program filled with brilliance and heart: Beethoven's Leonore Overture No. 3, a powerful tale of courage and triumph; Brahms's Variations on a Theme of Joseph Haydn, full of wit and wonder; and Schumann's radiant Symphony No. 2, music that soars with hope and resilience. Whether you're a longtime fan or new to orchestral music, this concert promises to captivate.

title: AlgoRhythms: AI, Music, and Human-Centered Creativity
location: Moot Courtroom, Baier Hall,211 S Indiana Ave, Bloomington, IN 47405, USA
description:
Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, partnered with the Maurer School of Law, IU Innovates, Music Tectonics, and the AI+Digital Futures Team, presents AlgoRhythms 2026, March 26–28 in Bloomington and online. This multi-day conference brings together artists, scholars, technologists, educators, and industry professionals to explore how artificial intelligence is transforming music creation, ownership, and the future of creative work.

Thursday, March 26
12:00 PM — Legal Trends in Music & AI
A discussion examining current legal developments shaping AI’s role in music.
Moot Courtroom, Maurer School of Law | Streaming online

1:30 PM — Legal Challenges and Opportunities in Music & AI
A panel exploring emerging legal questions around authorship, rights, and industry impact.
Moot Courtroom, Maurer School of Law | Streaming online

4:00 PM — Who Owns the Muse? Authenticity, Agency & AI in Music Creation
A conversation on creativity, authorship, and artistic identity in the age of AI.
Location TBA | Streaming online

Sign up here https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=NL4TEdGuAE2rS83QJRC-kdRT0NpCm_pFldrMPyyiXzVUMFRGSUg0V0lHV1BTTkJBSTVLOUFLUlRVMS4u&route=shorturl to attend either the in-person or virtual panel discussions.

location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: Come spend your Thursdays at the ACC practicing your Tagalog and celebrating Filipino culture! Every Thursday from 4:00-5:00 PM, we'll host a variety of language-immersive events—(included but not limited to) language table, speech practice, Tagalog tutoring, cooking workshops—to introduce the aspects that Filipino culture has to offer. Tagalog na Tayo! is open to everyone, whether you're a beginner, fluent speaker, or simply curious about Filipino culture.  All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11993690).

title: ACC Book Club features "To Save and Destroy" by Viet Nguyen
location: Asian Culture Center (807 E 10th street, Bloomington, IN)
description: What does it mean to be an "other"? How can writing shift the boundaries between insider and outsider for both author and audience? All are welcome to join the ACC in discussing these questions over dinner through Viet Thanh Nguyen's essay collection To Save and to Destroy: Writing as an Other. Originally delivered as the Norton Lecture series at Harvard in 2023-24, the six chapters span Nguyen's reflections on war, solidarity, and power, drawing from sources ranging from classics of American literature to Nguyen's own lived experience as a refugee. Digital copies of the eBook are freely available to access through IU Libraries, so all attendees are welcome to read ahead! Pizza will be served at each meeting. For more information, please reach out to ACC volunteer and book club moderator Cheryl Nong, PhD student in social psychology, at acc@iu.edu. The next book club meeting is on March 26, 2026.  All Indiana University events, groups, activities, and educational, cultural, and historical observances welcome all IU students and other members of the Indiana University community.For more information, view this event in BeInvolved (https://beinvolved.indiana.edu/event/11950464).
source: IU Asian Culture Center, IU beINvolved Student Orgs

title: From Indiana to Ambassador—Lessons from a Global Career ( A Global Competencies Skills Development Series)
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: 1060
description: Make your Foreign Service connection explicit.  A two-time U.S. Ambassador and Indiana native, Ambassador Maria Brewer (https://www.state.gov/biographies/maria-brewer) built her career in the U.S. Foreign Service, leading missions in Africa and South Asia and managing complex diplomatic negotiations.  If you are exploring a career in the Foreign Service or international policy, this is a rare opportunity for candid access. Hear how assignments are chosen, how reputations are built, and how leaders navigate pressure overseas.  Come with questions, leave with clarity about what this path demands and what it offers.Dinner will be provided.

title: You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before
description: Dive deep into the archival collections of IU Libraries Moving Image Archive through a new monthly screening series of short films! Each month, rare and unique archival films will be presented focusing on underrepresented narratives and underrepresented motion-picture practices that push on and expand the spectrum of what is considered "cinema."  In an effort to keep audiences open-minded and with limited pre-conceptions of these rare works of celluloid, descriptions of films to be shown will not be shared prior to screenings. Join us based on announced themes and the expectation that the works you see will fall outside of Hollywood tropes, outside of mainstream commercial expectations, and outside of conventional filmmaking practices. Trust us, you (probably) haven't seen these films before!  This program is curated by Essence London and hosted by the IU Libraries Moving Image Archive Screening Room in collaboration with the Black Film Center & Archive.  Please remember to register below.

title: You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before with IULMIA
description: For the past four years, the IU Libraries Moving Image Archive has hosted a regular screening series showcasing rare archival shorts that highlight underrepresented narratives and experimental motion-picture practices—works that expand the very idea of "cinema."On Thursday, March 26th at 5:30 p.m., the BFCA and IULMIA will present a collaborative edition of You (probably) Haven't Seen This Before, curated by Publications & Programming Associate Essence London. The event will be held in IULMIA's Screening Room and will last under an hour! Refreshments will be served following the screening.

title: Paying For It
description: $8.00 - Tickets for the 7 PM screening of PAYING FOR IT 2025 • Canada • 85 minutes • dir. Sook-Yin Lee Thursday, March 26th at Backspace Gallery ( 112 W 6th St ) $ 8 | All Ages Showtime: 7pm TRAILER Synopsis : Paying For It is a live-action adaptation of acclaimed alternative-cartoonist Chester Brown’s best-selling graphic novel. In the late 90s, Chester and Sonny are a long-term, committed, romantic couple. When Sonny wants to redefine their relationship, Chester, an introverted cartoonist, starts sleeping with sex workers and discovers a new kind of intimacy in the process. In a kind of double-act of portraiture, Sook-Yin Lee, Chester Brown’s real-life ex, adapts his graphic novel in a compassionate and unique movie. The result is a singular work of cinematic autofiction that is alive, funny and moving. The handling of sex and intimacy is deft, narrative and necessary. Nothing is hidden, but at the same time, never gratuitous. The humor is grounded and smart. Paying For It is an intelligent and affecting work about love, sex and non-monogamy for adults. The story is relevant and appeals to audiences to reconsider hetero-normative lifestyles.

title: Take Shelter w/ filmmaker | Transfixed: Michael Shannon on Screen, Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Series
location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description:
When Curtis LaForche (Michael Shannon) begins having nightmares of an encroaching, apocalyptic storm, he refrains from telling his wife, Samantha (Jessica Chastain). To protect her and their six-year-old Deaf daughter Hannah (Tova Stewart), Curtis starts focusing his anxiety and money into the obsessive building of a storm shelter. While Hannah's healthcare and special needs education has resulted in financial struggle, Curtis' seemingly inexplicable behavior concerns Samantha and provokes intolerance among co-workers, friends and neighbors. However, the resulting strain on his marriage and tension within the community doesn't compare to Curtis' private fear of what his disturbing dreams may truly signify. [121 min; drama, thriller; English]

A Q&A with actor Michael Shannon and Joshua Coonrod (Assistant Teaching Professor of English and Communication, Missouri University of Science and Technology) will follow the screening.

Lichens, which are a unique partnership between fungi and algae or cyanobacteria, are more than just “mossy stuff” on trees and rocks; they’re fascinating indicators of air quality, habitat diversity, and ecosystem health. As we hike, we’ll observe these often-overlooked organisms in their many forms and learn about their biology, where they live, and why they matter.

title: 21JPSI/IKS: "From Manners to Rules: Advocating for Legalism in South Korea and Japan" (Prof. Celeste Arrington, GWU)
location: GLOBAL & INTERNATIONAL STUDIES BUILDING - Room Number: Room 1112
description: This public event is open to all students, faculty, staff, and members of the community. Please join us! Professor Arrington's prepared remarks will be followed by a discussion/audience Q&A. Co-hosted with HLS' Institute for Korean Studies, and co-sponsored by the Department of Political Science and the Maurer School of Law's Stewart Center on the Global Legal Profession.About the talk:  Persons with disabilities have gained new rights and access in the past few decades in Japan and South Korea. Laws and regulations related to disabled persons have become more detailed and enforceable, and policymaking processes increasingly include persons with disabilities. Celeste Arrington unpacks how the demand for law is driving these advances in disability rights. By advocating for legislative changes and creatively using them, including through the courts, activists and lawyers are reshaping regulatory style and forging new opportunities for political engagement. The legalistic turn in disability-related policy marks a significant change in Japan and Korea, where officials relied on nonbinding guidelines, bureaucratic discretion, and vague laws. This talk draws on Arrington's new book, From Manners to Rules: Advocating for Legalism in South Korea and Japan (https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/from-manners-to-rules/F68A14FC5E643B2FE1DF3708EDAD4D79) (Cambridge, 2025).About the speaker:                      Professor Celeste Arrington (https://elliott.gwu.edu/celeste-arrington) specializes in comparative politics, with a regional focus on the Koreas and Japan. Her research interests include law and social change, governance, civil society, social movements, policy-making processes, lawyers, the media and politics, and qualitative methods. She is also interested in the international relations and security of Northeast Asia and transnational activism.                                        Professor Arrington is director of the GW Institute for Korean Studies (GWIKS) and co-director of the East Asia National Resources Center. She is also a Visiting Research Scholar at Princeton's School of Public and International Affairs and a faculty affiliate of the Global Japan Lab. Arrington received a Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley, an MPhil from the University of Cambridge, and an A.B. summa cum laude from Princeton University. She has been a fellow at Harvard, the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, the Law and Public Affairs Program at Princeton, and the Ludwig-Maximilians-University in Munich. GW's Office of the Vice President for Research awarded her the 2021 Early Career Research Scholar Award. Her article with Claudia Kim won the 2023 Asian Law and Society Association's distinguished article award.                  This event is made possible thanks to generous financial support from the Japan Foundation. All 21JPSI events are 100% in-person unless otherwise noted.
source: IU Hamilton Lugar School, IU Maurer School of Law

title: EASC Colloquium: Shang Wei
description: Professor Wei Shang specializes in pre-modern Chinese literature and culture, especially the fiction and drama of the Ming and Qing dynasties. His research interests also include print culture, book history and intellectual history of the same period. His book “Rulin Waishi" and Cultural Transformation in Late Imperial China (2003) addresses the role of Confucian ritualism and fiction in shaping the intellectual and cultural changes of the eighteenth century. His other publications are concerned with Jin Ping Mei Cihua (The Plum in the Golden Vase), late Ming culture, fiction commentary, and medieval poetry, including Writing on Landmarks: From Yellow Crane Tower to Phoenix Pavilion (2020). He is the editor and co-editor of several volumes in both English and Chinese, and a contributor to The Cambridge History of Chinese Literature (2010).

title: Burke Lecture Series: Dr. Sampada Aranke
location: SIDNEY AND LOIS ESKENAZI MUSEUM OF ART - Room Number: Martin Commons
description: This talk will consider the ways that famed Black American conceptual artist David Hammons cites, leverages, and operationalizes white, western canons to probe the generative limits of art historical discourse and canonization. The artist's intentional engagement with the practices and legacies of artists whose oeuvres have formed an avant-garde western canon like Duchamp, Klein, Matta-Clark, is not about mere adoration, but in fact a strategic assessment, if not deformation, of art historical discourses. Operating like a trojan horse, Hammons has seamlessly created opportunities for alignment and association, making it so that named collectors of Duchamp and museums that collect Matta-Clark have incomplete collections if they do not own a work by Hammons. This project will work alongside two key Hammons works, The Holy Bible: Old Testament (2002) and Days End (2021) to consider how the artist breaks disciplinary canons in order to make radically renewed associations.     Lecture will be held until 5 pm followed by questions. Reception to follow.Sponsored by the Robert E. & Avis Tarrant Burke Lecture Fund.

title: Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge
description: Date: March 27 to June 5, 2026 (not meeting on Good Friday)Tuition: $3,995*Duration: 10 week courseDelivery: Online sessions Fridays from 4 to 5 p.m. ET  Cyberattacks, ranging from hacktivists to organized crime networks and even nation states, are targeting vulnerable networks and are frequently successful in stealing funds as well as valuable intellectual property.  At the same time, there is a booming demand across both the public and private sectors for practitioners with this training, with more than 700,000 openings nationwide for cybersecurity professionals. There is a particular need for those with an expertise in space cybersecurity given the reliance on space-based infrastructure for everything from weather forecasting and satellite telecommunications to broadband internet.Since Russia's cyberattacks on space-based services (https://www.cigionline.org/publications/the-first-space-cyber-war-and-the-need-for-new-regimes-and-policies/?_gl=1*19p6xc0*_ga*MTY4ODYwMTMzNC4xNzQzNDI2Mjk5*_ga_61CH0D2DQW*MTc0MzY5MTM3Ni4yMi4xLjE3NDM2OTIxNDQuNjAuMC4w*_gcl_aw*R0NMLjE3NDMwMTI0MzMuQ2owS0NRandxSW1fQmhEbkFSSXNBS0JZY211dXhkT0M4ZWwza0tFMzUzSEpOTm5sOEJ6TThuUFRTRjRISEtYV0ZIRmExZThuTVBoQVN5d2FBbm5IRUFMd193Y0I.*_gcl_au*NjEyMjMwMTQ2LjE3NDM2OTEzNzc.) provided by commercial US space companies during its war on Ukraine, the cybersecurity of space systems has become high on the agenda, spurring discussions at the White House and in Congress, and a $700 million budget request by the Space Force (https://www.darkreading.com/cloud-security/us-space-force-wants-700m-cybersecurity) for this purpose alone. At the same time, a fast-growing market has emerged (https://www.inc.com/heather-wilde/harness-power-of-space-cybersecurity-for-growth-opportunity.html), with demand for professionals and firms.  Participants in the Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge program will gain in-depth understanding of the various cyber threats to space systems as well as techniques, tools, and best practices to address those threats. At the end of the program, participants will have the capability to develop and implement effective strategies for managing cyber risks to space systems.  After successful completion of an assurance of learning (assessment) at the conclusion of the program, participants will earn the Kelley School of Business Space Cybersecurity Digital Badge. Participants may also earn 3 credit hours toward the 12 credit hour Kelley School of Business Cybersecurity Management Graduate Certificate. The Cybersecurity Management Graduate Certificate (https://kelley.iu.edu/programs/executive-education-degrees-certificates/online-certificates/cybersecurity-management/index.html) may, in turn, be applied to the IU MS in Cybersecurity Risk Management (https://cyberrisk.iu.edu/?_gl=1*1833cq*_gcl_au*MTU3NDIzMTYyNy4xNzQ3MjMzNjYx*_ga*MTc4NDY5NDU2My4xNzMzNjM2NDc4*_ga_61CH0D2DQW*czE3NDcyMzA4OTkkbzIwJGcxJHQxNzQ3MjM2Mjc2JGo2MCRsMCRoMCRkMFdsRmYxSkwzaUs1bnoyYmNvdmVyY09qMlFscDFNV1R1QQ..).  CISA designation: Your participation in this program and completion of all course requirements earns you 12.0 CPE credits that can go towards the annual credits you need to maintain your CISA designation.  * Space ISAC members receive exclusive discounts of up to 50% based on membership tier. To learn about and obtain the discount, please reach out to Jade Maloney at jade@spaceisac.org (mailto:jade@spaceisac.org).  * GI/VA: We proudly welcome active-duty service members and veterans to the space cybersecurity program. Recognizing that GI Bill and VA education benefits do not apply to this program, IU is pleased to offer a Service Members & Veterans Scholarship amounting to 30% of the tuition. To receive the scholarship, please enter the code GI/VA30 during registration. Proof of military or veteran status should be provided by the start date of the program.

title: Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Presents: Michael Shannon, Brett Neveu, and "Eric LaRue" | Transfixed: Michael Shannon on Screen, Jorgensen Guest Filmmaker Series
location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description:
In Michael Shannon’s directorial debut—adapted from Brett Neveu’s 2002 renowned stage play—a mother struggles to cope in the aftermath of a shocking crime at the hands of her son. Janice (Judy Greer) is struggling, moving through life as if in a haze and unable to let go of her anger and frustration following her son's violent and shocking crimes. While her husband (Alexander Skarsgård) has found refuge at a new church, Janice finds it hard to seek solace in her faith despite her pastor’s pleas to heal her wounds by meeting with the mothers of her son’s victims. As Janice ponders her role in the community, she questions whether there is any established path to healing from an experience such as hers. Eric LaRue asks audiences to witness the frayed emotional ripples violent acts can engender. [119 min; drama; English]

Join award-winning writer Brett Neveu and award-winning actor/director Michael Shannon for an intimate and wide-ranging conversation on the art of collaboration and all points between. Please note this program will encompass a brief onstage conversation, followed by the film and then a post-film Q&A.

title: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead by Tom Stoppard
description: Acclaimed as a modern dramatic masterpiece, Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead is the fabulously inventive tale of Hamlet as told from the worm's-eye view of the bewildered Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, two minor characters in Shakespeare's play. In Tom Stoppard's best-known work, this Shakespearean Laurel and Hardy finally get a chance to take the lead role, but do so in a world where echoes of Waiting for Godot resound, where reality and illusion intermix, and where fate leads our two heroes to a tragic but inevitable end.

Dan Brown’s international best-seller comes to life on stage in this thrilling, high-stakes adventure! About Making Movies
Making Movies (3/25/26 – 3/27/26) A dynamic band that blends Afro-Latino rhythms and rock ’n’ roll with African-derived percussion and styles like rumba, merengue, mambo, and cumbia. Founded by Enrique Chi—a Panamanian songwriter, musician, filmmaker, and cultural producer—the Latin Grammy-nominated band serves as the primary platform for Chi’s creative mission. Armed with electric guitars and a mejoranera, a folkloric guitar native to Panamá, the band interweaves styles as divergent as cumbia and blues, salsa, soul, and rock’n’roll––all boiled down into their own original sound.

title: Norman Krieger, piano; Nicholas Tzavaras, cello; Jason Posnock, violin
description: Repertoire  Beethoven: Piano Trio in D Major, Op. 70 No. 1 (1808) ("Ghost")Brahms: Piano Trio No. 2 in C Major, Op. 87 (1882)About the ArtistsNorman Krieger is Dean Charles H. Webb Chair in Music, professor of music in piano, and chair of the Piano Department at the Indiana University Jacobs School of Music, having previously served as professor at the University of Southern California Thornton School of Music. A native of Los Angeles, he is one of the most acclaimed pianists of his generation and is highly regarded as an artist of depth, sensitivity, and virtuosic flair. Krieger has collaborated with such notable conductors as Myung-Whun Chung, Donald Runnicles, Leonard Slatkin, and Michael Tilson Thomas. He regularly appears with the major orchestras of North America, among them the New York Philharmonic, Los Angeles Philharmonic, and Chicago Symphony. He has performed throughout Europe, Asia, and South America. In 2014, he recorded the Brahms Sonata Op. 1 and the Piano Concerto No. 2 with the London Symphony Orchestra under Philip Ryan Mann (Decca). In recital, Krieger has appeared throughout the United States, Europe, Mexico, and Asia. Chamber music collaborations have included appearances with Sheri Greenawald, Paul Huang, Sarah Chang, Pamela Frank, and the Tokyo String Quartet, among many others. His debut at New York City's prestigious Carnegie Hall and Mostly Mozart Festival earned him an invitation to Lincoln Center's Great Performers Series, and he was Gold Medal Winner of the first Palm Beach Invitational Piano Competition. Krieger began his studies with Esther Lipton. At age 15, he became a full-scholarship student of Adele Marcus at The Juilliard School, where he earned bachelor's and master's degrees. Subsequently, he studied with Alfred Brendel and Maria Curcio in London and earned an Artist Diploma from the New England Conservatory, where he worked with Russell Sherman. A champion of contemporary music, he features among his repertoire the music of such composers as John Adams, Leonard Bernstein, and John Corigliano. Krieger is founding artistic director of The Prince Albert Music Festival in Hawaii. Since 2008, he has served on the summer faculty at the Brevard Music Festival in North Carolina.  Violinist Jason Posnock is the president and CEO of the Brevard Music Center. He has enjoyed an international career as a concert violinist, educator, and arts administrator. His diverse performance portfolio includes serving for 17 years as concertmaster of the Asheville Symphony, a multiyear association with the Pittsburgh Symphony, and appearances as soloist, chamber musician, and orchestral principal in venues throughout the United States, United Kingdom, and India. Originally from New Jersey, Posnock holds degrees from Princeton University, Royal College of Music, Carnegie Mellon University, and an M.B.A. from the University of Nebraska. Posnock resides in Brevard, North Carolina, with his wife, flutist Dilshad Posnock, and their two children, Max and Lylah.  A native of East Harlem, cellist Nicholas Tzavaras has enjoyed a distinguished career as a soloist, chamber musician, and educator. From 2000 to 2024, he served as the cellist of the internationally acclaimed Shanghai Quartet, performing in more than 35 countries and appearing at major venues including Carnegie Hall, the Kennedy Center, Wigmore Hall, and the Seoul Arts Center. During his tenure, the quartet held long-standing residencies at Montclair State University and The Tianjin Juilliard School and became widely recognized for championing core classical repertoire as well as contemporary commissions. Tzavaras has recorded more than 28 albums on the Naxos, Delos, Decca, BIS, Centaur, Camerata, and New Albion labels. He also served as guest principal cellist with the Shanghai Symphony Orchestra from 2009 to 2024. He began his musical studies at age two on the violin with his mother, Roberta Guaspari, the pioneering educator whose work was chronicled in the Academy Award-nominated documentary Small Wonders and portrayed by Meryl Streep in the film Music of the Heart. He later shifted to the cello, attended the Bronx High School of Science, and earned degrees from the New England Conservatory and Stony Brook University. He also holds an M.B.A. from Montclair State University. A passionate and sought after educator, Tzavaras has held faculty and leadership roles at The Tianjin Juilliard School, Montclair State University, the Longy School of Music, and the University of Richmond. He currently serves as senior director of Artistic Planning and Educational Programs at the Brevard Music Center and is on the faculty of Brevard College. He lives in Asheville, North Carolina, with his wife, Sophia, and their three children.

location: IU Cinema,East 7th Street, Bloomington, IN, USA
description: Michael Shannon and Ashley Judd give searing performances in this skin-crawling exercise in fear and paranoia from director William Friedkin. Oklahoma waitress Aggie (Judd) lives a largely solitary life in a rundown motel. She's haunted by a tragic past and hounded by her ex-con ex-husband (Harry Connick, Jr.). When she sparks a romance with an eccentric drifter, Peter Evans (Shannon), she starts to feel hopeful again—until Peter reveals he is a victim of government experimentation. The couple begins to obsess over the nightmarish idea that they are infected by insects. Shannon reprises the role he originated in playwright Tracy Letts' original London production of the same name. [102 min; drama, thriller, horror; English]
